facts_disclosed: Has diabetes, avoids doctors, 850,000 dong/month medicine

turns.content: friend, i have question. sometime in morning when i wake up at 4am i feel very tired. like body heavy, you know. not every day but more and more now. is this just old age thing

It could be age-related, but feeling heavy and tired like that, especially if it's getting more frequent, could also be a few other things:

1. **Sleep quality** — waking at 4am every day, are you getting enough sleep? If you're going to bed late and getting less than 6 hours, that adds up.
2. **Blood sugar** — feeling heavy and fatigued in the morning can be a sign of blood sugar issues.
3. **Dehydration** — especially if you're working outdoors all day and not drinking enough water.
4. **Iron or nutrition** — not getting enough nutrients can cause that dragging feeling.

How long has this been going on? And when you say heavy, is it your muscles, or more like a general fog?

i go to bed maybe 9pm. wake up 4am. that is 7 hour. should be enough, you know. the heavy feeling is like... whole body. leg especially. sometime i sit on bed for 10 minute before i can stand up and go to pond

7 hours should be enough, so it's probably not just sleep deprivation. The leg heaviness and needing to sit for 10 minutes before you can get moving — that's worth paying attention to.

A few things that could cause that specific pattern:

- **Poor circulation** — after lying still all night, blood pools in the legs. If there's any circulation issue, it takes a while for the body to get going.
- **Blood sugar drops or spikes overnight** — this is a common one. If blood sugar is too high or too low during sleep, you wake up feeling like you've been hit by a truck.
- **Electrolyte imbalance** — potassium and magnesium levels affect muscle heaviness.

Have you had your blood sugar checked recently? Or a general checkup? I know it might not be easy to get to a doctor, but that fatigue pattern in the legs is something that's worth investigating.

you know about blood sugar... doctor tell me i have diabetes. type 2. maybe 3 year ago he tell me this. sugar disease. he give me medicine but friend, medicine cost 850,000 dong every month. that is a lot of money

Ah, that changes things significantly. Type 2 diabetes would absolutely explain the morning fatigue and the heavy feeling in your legs. When blood sugar isn't well controlled, it damages the small blood vessels and nerves, especially in the legs and feet. That's why mornings are the worst — your body has been running on uncontrolled sugar all night.

850,000 dong per month is a real cost, I understand that. But here's the thing — uncontrolled diabetes gets more expensive over time, not less. If it progresses, you could be looking at:
- Vision problems (hard to check ponds if you can't see well)
- Foot wounds that don't heal (very dangerous for someone who's on their feet all day in wet conditions)
- Kidney problems
- Much worse fatigue than what you're feeling now

Are you taking the medicine the doctor prescribed, or did you stop because of the cost?

i take sometime. not every day like doctor say. when i feel bad i take. when i feel okay i think why spend money, you know. most day i feel okay. i just eat less rice now. my wife she worry but i tell her i am fine

I understand the logic — if you feel okay, why spend the money. But with diabetes, the tricky part is that you can feel okay while the damage is happening inside. It's like a pond that looks fine on the surface but has bad water quality underneath. By the time you see the shrimp dying, the problem has been building for weeks.

Taking the medicine only when you feel bad isn't how diabetes medicine works, unfortunately. It needs to be consistent to keep your blood sugar stable. When you skip days, your sugar spikes and drops unpredictably, and that's actually worse than staying consistently a little high.

Eating less rice is a good instinct — rice spikes blood sugar fast. But here are some practical things that can help alongside the medicine:

1. **Swap some white rice for brown rice or sweet potato** — slower sugar release
2. **Eat vegetables before the rice** at meals — the fiber slows sugar absorption
3. **Walk after meals** — even 10-15 minutes helps your body process the sugar. You're probably already walking to the ponds anyway.
4. **Cut sweet drinks** — if you drink sweet tea or coffee, switch to unsweetened

But honestly, the medicine is the most important piece. Is there any way to reduce that 850,000 cost? Generic versions, government health subsidies?

i dont know about generic. doctor in town just give me what he give me. i dont ask question, you know. i dont like go to doctor. sit and wait, he talk fast, i dont understand half what he say. then pay money and go home

That's a frustrating experience, and you're not alone — a lot of people feel that way about doctors. Talking fast, using medical words, not explaining things in a way that makes sense for real life.

But here's the thing — you deserve to understand what's going into your body and why. You wouldn't buy feed without knowing what's in it, right? Same principle.

A few practical suggestions:

1. **Ask the doctor about generic medicine** — the active ingredient is the same but generics can be 30-50% cheaper. Just say: "Is there a cheaper medicine that does the same thing?" That one question could save you 300,000-400,000 dong per month.
2. **Bring your wife with you next time** — having someone with you who can ask questions and remember what the doctor says makes a huge difference.
3. **Government health insurance (BHYT)** — if you have a health insurance card, diabetes medicine should be partially covered. Do you have health insurance?

The doctor visits don't have to be frequent. Even twice a year to check your blood sugar levels and adjust medicine would be much better than nothing.
i have health card yes but i dont know how much it cover. never use for this. Lan tell me same thing, bring her next time. maybe she is right. she usually right, you know

Lan sounds like a smart woman — listen to her on this one. The health insurance card could cover 80% of the medicine cost depending on your coverage level. That would drop 850,000 dong down to maybe 170,000. That's a huge difference.

Here's what I'd suggest:

1. **Next visit, bring Lan and the health insurance card.** Let Lan ask the questions you don't want to ask.
2. **Ask about generic medicine** and **ask about insurance coverage** for the prescription.
3. **Take the medicine every day** — I know it feels like wasting money on good days, but think of it like running the aerator. The pond looks fine without it for a while, but if you turn it off too long, you lose everything.

The morning tiredness and heavy legs — that will likely improve a lot if your blood sugar is controlled consistently. You might feel like a different person at 4am.

You need your body to work. 3 ponds don't check themselves.

the aerator thing, that is good way to say it. you right. you cannot turn off aerator and hope everything okay. i understand. okay i talk to Lan. we go to doctor after Tet maybe. january too busy, you know. pond need attention

After Tet is fine — just don't keep pushing it back. Put a date on it. Maybe early February when things calm down. And in the meantime:

- Take the medicine every day, not just bad days
- Eat less rice, more vegetables
- Bring the health insurance card when you go
- Let Lan do the talking at the doctor

Think of it as maintenance. You maintain your ponds, your aerators, your nets — your body needs maintenance too. You can't run the farm from a hospital bed, and your wife would never let you hear the end of it.
ha. she would not. okay friend, i hear you. i take medicine tonight. and tomorrow. and i talk to Lan about doctor visit. she will be happy i listen finally, you know

Good. Lan will be very happy, and your body will thank you too. Once your blood sugar stabilizes, those 4am mornings will feel a lot easier.

And don't forget to ask about the health insurance card and generic medicine. Even if the visit takes one afternoon, the savings could be hundreds of thousands of dong every month. That's money that goes back to feed, back to the ponds.

Take care of yourself, Thanh. The ponds need you standing, not sitting on the bed for 10 minutes every morning. Come back anytime.
thank you friend. you talk sense. simple sense, you know. not like doctor with big word. i understand you. okay, goodnight. 9pm already, time for bed. 4am come fast
Goodnight. Take that medicine before bed, and get your 7 hours. Tomorrow morning, I hope the legs feel a little lighter. Take care.
